does: This paper introduces Sequential Preference Optimization (SPO), a method for aligning large language models with multiple dimensions of human preferences without explicit reward modeling. Rather than training separate reward models for different preference dimensions like helpfulness and harmlessness, SPO sequentially fine-tunes LLMs to optimize for each dimension in turn. The authors derive closed-form solutions for the optimal SPO policy and loss function, and provide gradient analysis explaining how the method preserves alignment on previously optimized dimensions while adding new preference constraints. Empirical evaluation across models of varying sizes and multiple benchmarks shows SPO substantially outperforms baseline approaches in achieving multi-dimensional alignment.
connects: This work builds on recent advances in preference-based alignment methods like Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) and reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF), which have moved away from training explicit reward models toward direct policy optimization. However, existing approaches typically handle single preference dimensions or require complex multi-reward-model management. SPO extends these ideas by addressing the challenge of sequential multi-objective alignment, drawing on optimization literature around multi-task learning and constrained policy optimization. The paper implicitly responds to limitations in methods like Bradley-Terry preference modeling and pairwise ranking approaches, proposing instead a sequential framework that avoids the computational and conceptual complexity of managing multiple reward functions simultaneously.
enables: SPO opens pathways for more sophisticated preference alignment in LLMs by providing a scalable approach to handle nuanced, multi-faceted human values. The method could enable development of models that seamlessly balance competing preferences without degradation on previously-learned dimensions, facilitating more practical deployment in real-world applications requiring strict safety-helpfulness tradeoffs. Future work could extend sequential alignment to continuous preference streams rather than discrete dimensions, develop theoretical guarantees on preference preservation across longer sequences, or combine SPO with other alignment techniques. The implicit reward modeling approach may inspire alternatives to explicit value functions across other areas of preference learning and human-AI interaction.
